title: Rate of child deaths attributed to child and maternal malnutrition
subtitle: The estimated number of deaths attributed to child and maternal malnutrition, per 100,000 children under the age of 5.
caption:
Rate of child deaths attributed to child and maternal malnutrition, 2023

The estimated number of deaths attributed to child and maternal malnutrition, per 100,000 children under the age of 5.

This choropleth map displays the estimated mortality rate for children under five attributed to malnutrition across the globe in 2023, measured as deaths per 100,000 children. The visualization employs a continuous color scale ranging from light beige to deep maroon, where darker shades correspond to higher mortality rates. The legend defines specific bands starting at 0 and extending through 10, 20, 50, 100, 200, 500, 1,000, and up to 2,000 deaths per 100,000. The highest values, represented by the darkest maroon colors falling in the bands above 1,000, are concentrated in a contiguous region of sub-Saharan Africa. Countries including Chad, the Central African Republic, Somalia, and Nigeria appear in these darkest bands, indicating rates exceeding 1,000 deaths per 100,000 children. Surrounding nations in West and East Africa, such as Mali, Burkina Faso, and Ethiopia, display slightly lighter but still intense red shades, placing them in the 500 to 1,000 range. In contrast, the lowest rates are visible across North America, Western Europe, Australia, and parts of South America like Chile and Argentina, where the map shows light beige colors corresponding to the 0 to 10 band. Intermediate values appear in parts of South Asia and Southeast Asia; India and Pakistan show orange hues consistent with the 50 to 200 range, while Indonesia and the Philippines fall in lighter orange bands between 20 and 50. Greenland and several island nations are marked with diagonal hatching, indicating no data is available for these territories. The geographic distribution shows a clear concentration of the highest mortality rates in the central belt of the African continent, while the lowest rates cluster in high-income regions of the Northern Hemisphere and Oceania.
